items.q: What is the difference between constructive and destructive waves?
items.correct: Constructive waves have strong swash and weak backwash, building beaches; destructive waves have weak swash and strong backwash, dragging sediment offshore and eroding cliffs.
items.misconception: Big waves are destructive and small waves are constructive.
items.random_wrong: Constructive waves build sandcastles and destructive waves wash them away.
items.why: It's the swash-to-backwash balance, not size, that classifies waves. Tall, plunging waves are destructive; long, spilling waves are constructive. Wave frequency also matters.

items.q: Why is the global energy transition slower than many expect?
items.correct: Existing fossil-fuel infrastructure is sunk cost, low-carbon alternatives need vast capital, intermittency requires storage, and political/economic interests resist change — even as costs of renewables fall.
items.misconception: The energy transition is slow because renewable energy doesn't actually work.
items.random_wrong: The transition is slow because people don't want to give up cars.
items.why: Renewables are now often cheaper than fossil fuels per unit. The barriers are infrastructure, finance, grid integration and politics — a system inertia problem, not a technology limitation.

items.q: How are fold mountains like the Himalayas formed?
items.correct: By the collision of two continental plates (India and Eurasia) where neither can subduct, so the crust crumples and is forced upwards over millions of years.
items.misconception: Fold mountains form when one plate slides under another into the mantle.
items.random_wrong: Fold mountains form from giant volcanoes erupting upwards.
items.why: Students confuse continent-continent collision with subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, so it crumples — distinct from oceanic-continental boundaries like the Andes.

items.q: What is the Hubbert curve about?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peak oil. “Population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: South America. “Africa” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What % of world lithium is in the Lithium Triangle?
items.why: Exact answer: 60 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
